caveats: a: The available industry-specific occupation profile is old, covers NAICS 71213 rather than a modern six-digit private-only population, and reports broad EEO categories rather than task shares.
a: ILO exposure is not adoption or job displacement and is not specific to US visitor attractions.
a: The injected compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis, as stated in the assignment.
rho: The clearest zoo implementation source is an AZA commercial-member feature and may select successful adopters.
rho: General occupational exposure does not establish feasibility under zoo accreditation, animal-welfare, or public-sector controls.
e: The ownership statistic is decades old and based on an acknowledged incomplete national universe.
e: AZA membership is neither a census of NAICS 712130 nor an ownership classification.
e: The injected firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE; eligibility is applied to that uncertain base without re-estimating it.
s5: The owner-readiness survey is cross-industry, self-reported, and not representative of this NAICS.
s5: The sell-through statistic refers to businesses that go to market and is not a direct annual hazard for eligible firms.
s5: Internal succession is excluded unless it constitutes a qualifying control transfer under the screen.
q: The most detailed revenue mix is Canadian and nonprofit, not the US private eligible subset.
q: The estimate separates price retention from attendance volume, but actual institutions may reinvest savings in animal welfare or visitor experience rather than report margin.
d5: BLS employment projections are not demand projections and cover many animal-care settings outside zoos.
d5: AZA attendance commentary is not a quantified, private-only longitudinal series and may reflect large accredited institutions.
d5: Demand is discretionary and vulnerable to recession, weather, disease, travel disruption, and competing leisure formats.
o: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured operator-requirement share.
o: The estimate could be lower for education-only and simple exhibit formats than for complex living collections.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily consumer admissions and memberships, supplemented by school and public programs, events, retail and food, donations, grants, and public support; prices are generally posted per visit or membership rather than cost-plus.
lens.excluded_activities: Government departments, captive exhibits, non-operating foundations, conservation land without a live public collection, shell entities, and facilities whose economics cannot support a transferable operating enterprise.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators whose recurring or repeat external-customer service is paid access, membership, educational programming, events, or related visitor services centered on live animal or plant collections.

a.bridge: The 2007 EEOC profile measures employment counts by broad job category for 50 units in NAICS 71213, while ILO measures task-level GenAI exposure across occupations globally. I treat office/clerical, sales, management, professional, and selected technical work as more exposed than animal care, grounds, craft, and other physical service work, then make a wage-weighted judgment for current US 712130. The old occupational mix may understate today's digital administration, while generic GenAI exposure may overstate substitution in safety- and mission-sensitive workflows.
caveats: The available industry-specific occupation profile is old, covers NAICS 71213 rather than a modern six-digit private-only population, and reports broad EEO categories rather than task shares.
ILO exposure is not adoption or job displacement and is not specific to US visitor attractions.
The injected compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis, as stated in the assignment.
a.change_evidence: A current six-digit OEWS staffing and wage extract joined to task-level O*NET data, plus time-and-motion studies from a representative set of private zoos and gardens separating animal/plant care from ticketing, development, education, and administration.
a.rationale: Admissions and membership servicing, scheduling, marketing content, donor communications, bookkeeping, reporting, and first-line visitor support contain document- and data-heavy tasks. In contrast, feeding, cleaning, horticulture, animal observation, training, facilities work, and much face-to-face interpretation remain physical or accountable-human work. The resulting exposed wage share is meaningful but clearly a minority of compensation.

d5.bridge: BLS projects US animal-care and service employment to grow 11% from 2024 to 2034, but that occupation includes pet services and other settings; a five-year equivalent is only a directional demand anchor. AZA reports strong attendance recovery, often above pre-pandemic levels, for its community. I temper both signals for discretionary leisure exposure and measure constant-price, constant-quality service quantity rather than employment or nominal revenue. The broad BLS occupation likely overstates zoo demand growth, while rebound commentary may overstate trend growth after a temporary shock.
caveats: BLS employment projections are not demand projections and cover many animal-care settings outside zoos.
AZA attendance commentary is not a quantified, private-only longitudinal series and may reflect large accredited institutions.
Demand is discretionary and vulnerable to recession, weather, disease, travel disruption, and competing leisure formats.
d5.change_evidence: A matched panel of US private 712130 admissions, member visits, school-group attendance, and constant-dollar program units from 2019 through 2026, plus institution-level five-year attendance forecasts and local demographic exposure.
d5.rationale: Live attractions retain education, recreation, conservation, and community uses, with repeat attendance and memberships supporting a roughly stable-to-modestly-growing service basket. Household leisure competition, weather, travel cycles, and affordability can produce declines, so the range crosses 1.0.

e.bridge: A historical US museum-related survey found only about 8% of covered botanical gardens and zoos/aquariums were operated by companies, businesses, or individuals, with most run by government, nonprofit, or educational bodies. I use that as a floor-like ownership indicator and allow a higher present-day share for private wildlife attractions and gardens, then exclude entities without transferable control or a repeat external-customer operating model. The 1970-era sample and incomplete universe create large two-sided error.
caveats: The ownership statistic is decades old and based on an acknowledged incomplete national universe.
AZA membership is neither a census of NAICS 712130 nor an ownership classification.
The injected firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE; eligibility is applied to that uncertain base without re-estimating it.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-level linkage of Census Business Register legal form, IRS exempt status, USDA exhibitor records, state botanical-garden registries, and beneficial ownership, filtered to the injected $1-10M EBITDA band.
e.rationale: The operating category includes many municipal and nonprofit institutions whose assets and governance cannot be bought like an ordinary company. A smaller private subset can meet the lens through repeat admissions, memberships, events, and programs and can support a transferable enterprise.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 712130 ($2,102,563,624) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 712130 ($5,606,441,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $143,740 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 712130); class <5: 309 firms, avg revenue/firm $351,984 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,570 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 98 firms, avg revenue/firm $836,663 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $139,221 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 106 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,532,670 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $255,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 173 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,583,289 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $929,059 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 89 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,381,674 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,388,311 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,667,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,755,838 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 89

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured operator-requirement share.
The estimate could be lower for education-only and simple exhibit formats than for complex living collections.
o.change_evidence: Visitor substitution surveys comparing virtual and physical experiences, program-level unit economics for remote education, longitudinal self-service adoption, and closure/conversion studies identifying which current service quantities disappear rather than merely change channel.
o.rationale: The defining service requires custody and welfare of live collections, enclosure or garden maintenance, on-site safety, and a credible accountable institution. Digital content, remote learning, self-service ticketing, and software can displace slices of distribution and administration, but cannot eliminate the operator responsible for animals, plants, facilities, and the physical visitor experience.

q.bridge: Canada's 2023 survey directly measures the revenue mix of not-for-profit zoos and botanical gardens: earned revenue exceeded unearned revenue, with admissions, goods and services, and memberships prominent. US museum evidence also identifies admissions and ancillary purchases as important. I map this posted-price and membership mix to retention of an implemented benefit over five years. Canadian nonprofit/public funding and US private LMM economics differ; mission-driven pricing may cause more sharing than a for-profit operator would choose.
caveats: The most detailed revenue mix is Canadian and nonprofit, not the US private eligible subset.
The estimate separates price retention from attendance volume, but actual institutions may reinvest savings in animal welfare or visitor experience rather than report margin.
q.change_evidence: Contract and price-history review for a sample of private LMM institutions, including admission and membership renewals, school-group agreements, concession arrangements, public subsidies, discounting, attendance elasticity, and competitor price responses after documented automation.
q.rationale: Posted admissions, membership, event, and program prices do not mechanically rebate lower back-office costs, so a well-run operator can retain much of an implemented benefit. Over renewals and competitive price comparisons, some value leaks through discounts, improved visitor service, wages, donor expectations, and restrained pricing, while government and grant-funded revenue offers little direct monetization.

rho.bridge: AZA examples document cloud consolidation and reduced administrative overhead in member institutions, while ILO documents that clerical exposure is high but full automation remains limited. I translate these observed adjacent workflow changes into a five-year implementation share for the narrower set of AI-exposed tasks. Vendor-sponsored examples likely overstate attainable implementation; accreditation, animal welfare, safety, fragmented data, and limited budgets pull the estimate down.
caveats: The clearest zoo implementation source is an AZA commercial-member feature and may select successful adopters.
General occupational exposure does not establish feasibility under zoo accreditation, animal-welfare, or public-sector controls.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after payroll hours, error rates, and service levels from 10-20 small and midsized US institutions implementing AI-enabled CRM, admissions, finance, and guest-support systems, with workflows audited for actual headcount avoidance.
rho.rationale: Ticketing, membership, scheduling, standard communications, analytics, and reporting can be integrated into existing systems within five years. Smaller institutions' thin technical teams, disconnected systems, donor and public accountability, safeguarding, and the need for expert review of animal, plant, and educational content keep a substantial part of exposed work from becoming operational labor substitution.

s5.bridge: The Exit Planning Institute surveyed 1,162 US business owners across more than 25 industries, not zoo and garden operators: 49% wanted to exit within five years, while its broader readiness material says only 20-30% of businesses taken to market actually sell. I combine intent and observed-market attrition as an anchor, then lower the midpoint for specialized collections, real estate, permits, mission constraints, and a thin buyer set. Survey self-selection likely raises exit intent; internal family transfers and failed sales mean intent overstates qualifying control transfers.
caveats: The owner-readiness survey is cross-industry, self-reported, and not representative of this NAICS.
The sell-through statistic refers to businesses that go to market and is not a direct annual hazard for eligible firms.
Internal succession is excluded unless it constitutes a qualifying control transfer under the screen.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed US transactions from DealStats, GF Data, BizBuySell, PitchBook, and state ownership or USDA-license changes, manually classified to 712130 and separating arm's-length control transfers from nonprofit reorganizations and internal succession.
s5.rationale: Among the already-eligible private operators, owner aging creates real transfer supply, but a qualifying five-year control sale is much less common than stated exit intent. Collection stewardship, licenses, site-specific assets, and limited strategic buyers constrain completion.

principal_driver: Repeat posted-price visitor and membership revenue can retain savings from automating administrative and commercial workflows.
principal_weakness: Most recognizable institutions are not conventionally transferable private companies, and the live-collection core is difficult to automate.
